content:
Hi everyone. Sorry if this is an already tackled issue, but I'm new here. I don't even know how to use Zulip. I'm just very exhausted and frustrated. I've built a kernel module in C before, but now I want to do one in rust. It's not even my end goal, but everything I'm trying isn't working for me. I just need someone to guide me through the exact steps to take. No documentation has been helpful.

I found this video on YouTube from the Linux Foundation: "Mentorship Session: Writing Linux Kernel Modules in Rust". Apparently it's no longer useful. I tried to follow his steps to no avail.

He used the 'rust' branch from the repo. So I did, but when building, I got a warning about 'bindgen' being newer, then an error message about some missing types when generating the bindings. I tried installing the version of bindgen it wanted, still got errors. Okay, later I read more of the docs, and it looked like that 'rust' branch is deprecated or something, so I should really use the 'rust-next' branch. Okay.

I cloned that branch, now I'm running the "allnoconfig qemu-busybox-min.config rust.config" command, and I get this: "qemu-busybox-min.config fragment does not exists on this architecture.  Stop." I try searching in the repo's issues for a similar problem, and I saw this response to someone's question with the same problem. Very unhelpful.

Now I just wanted to forget qemu and all the ceremony. The aim is to just start writing something in vscode with completions. I don't know how I can restructure the make command without the qemu, but I just omit that part and see what happens. Command successful. Now I want to create the rust-analyzer config. I run the "make LLVM=1 rust-analyzer" command, and I get this: "No rule to make target 'rust-analyzer'.  Stop."

I've not been able to get this rust-analyzer command to work since I started this whole thing. At least, if I could just get it set up to start writing the module in vscode with completions, I can worry about the build later. I'm just exhausted.

If someone has followed that video, and is more experienced with this, could you please tell me how to just replicate the video with the current state of the repo? I just need to get started. This isn't even a full recount of all the errors I've encountered. I can't even remember some.

Also when replying to this, kindly do it in a way I can get a prominent notification for. As I said, I'm new to Zulip. And I can't even think straight at this point.
If this will help, I use Arch with this uname -a output: Linux <user> 6.17.8-arch1-1 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Fri, 14 Nov 2025 06:54:20 +0000 x86_64 GNU/Linux

You said you've built a C kernel module before. How did you run the kernel when you did so? There aren't any Rust-specific instructions for how to run the kernel because there is nothing specific to Rust about it. You should be able to run a kernel with Rust enabled in the same way as how you run a kernel without it.

As for choice of branch, the 'rust' branch is from back before Rust became part of the kernel proper. Today, you can use the usual tree from Linus.

Alice Ryhl said:

You said you've built a C kernel module before. How did you run the kernel when you did so? There aren't any Rust-specific instructions for how to run the kernel because there is nothing specific to Rust about it. You should be able to run a kernel with Rust enabled in the same way as how you run a kernel without it.

As for choice of branch, the 'rust' branch is from back before Rust became part of the kernel proper. Today, you can use the usual tree from Linus with Rust.

I did that by actually building it for my running system. I think I was using Ubuntu then. I just write the module and use this makefile:

obj-m = mymodule.o

all:
    make -C /lib/modules/$(shell uname -r)/build M=$(shell pwd) modules

clean:
    make -C /lib/modules/$(shell uname -r)/build M=$(shell pwd) clean

After that I just load the module into the running system. Done.

I tried to do the same with my current system, but I was overwhelmed with so many caveats. My installed linux-headers package was missing some files required. Like when I try the make rust-analyzer in the /lib/modules/.../build directory, it starts looking for files in arm/. e.g.: can't open file "arch/arm/crypto/Kconfig". I'm on x64. It looks like building with rust needs the full kernel source even for out-of-tree modules. That's why I had to go through this whole thing. Ideally I'd have liked to build it so that I can insert it into my running system. It just doesn't seem that simple. So at least, I've found a tutorial I can follow, but even that isn't helpful.

I'm not experienced with kernel programming. I'm new to it. But I've been a programmer for long. Just that this kernel building setup and tree and config and stuff is a lot. And worse with Rust. I understand it's still an ongoing thing, so support will be better in the future. But it's like every rust module tutorial I come across, I'm not able to implement.

Forgive my long talk. Because of how frustrated I am, I wish I could just be given the steps to follow. Once I clone the Rust-for-linux/linux repo on the rust-next branch, what do I do next, if I just want to build a simple hello-world kernel module.
What happens if you run make LLVM=1 in your checkout?
I don't think there is any recent tutorial like the video you shared a link to, unfortunately.

Alice Ryhl said:

make LLVM=1

Just tried it. I get asked some questions, but I just press enter to take the defaults. Then I got this: xargs: llvm-ar: No such file or directory. This is even a new error.
That error means you need to install the tool called llvm-ar to build the kernel.

A quick google search gave me these commands in the case of arch:

sudo pacman -S llvm clang gnu-efi base-devel

But I don't actually know which tools are required in your case. The Rust-specific tools are listed here:
https://docs.kernel.org/rust/quick-start.html

But the llvm-ar tool is just something all kernel builds needs. It's not a Rust-specific requirement.

If you want to run the kernel directly on your own system, then you're going to have to compile your own kernel too. I would start by searching for instructions for how to compile your own kernel for Arch Linux specifically, completely ignoring anything related to Rust. Once you have that working, you can try to enable Rust on the system.

Actually I think the configuration used by Arch Linux already enables Rust by default. So if you follow instructions for how to build your own Arch Linux kernel, that should give you Rust support for free.

Okay, I realised the llvm-ar is part of llvm, which wasn't installed (I don't know how much of a rookie mistake that is). I've just been trying to conservatively follow the documentations I found. I've installedllvm. And it's currently still on the make LLVM=1 command.

Alice Ryhl said:

If you want to run the kernel directly on your own system, then you're going to have to compile your own kernel too. I would start by searching for instructions for how to compile your own kernel for Arch Linux specifically, completely ignoring anything related to Rust. Once you have that working, you can try to enable Rust on the system.

Thing is, I didn't want to go through the trouble of building a whole kernel and stuff. It's just a lot, and takes away from my whole purpose. Very likely, it's going to require so many things, and I'm going to run into a lot of errors. Because I've already built a kernel module in C, and all I required was the linux headers, I just wanted to do the same thing.

Building a Rust module in that way is not easy. For example, it requires that you use exactly the same rustc version as Arch Linux used when they compiled your kernel. And if you run into anything missing in the core kernel, you're out of luck. Using Rust in the way you described is unfortunately not well supported at this time.

Exactly. And I found out that even if I get the full source code, not every distribution supports it still, so my best bet is to clone the linux fork in the rust-for-linux project, becasue they've put things in place for building with rust. And there's where I'm finding issues.

In my mind, what the video did made complete sense: Clone the rust-for-linux project (it's a full linux kernel source, with rust features supported), build a kernel module against that version, then run the whole thing in a virtual machine. It worked for him in the video. But I can't even do the same thing here. With the full source code.

Anyway, my make LLVM=1 command has completed. Any suggestions as to the next step?

Sometimes I use qemu to run the kernel tests. You could give that a try as a starting point. The command I use is:

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64 --kconfig_add CONFIG_RUST=y

Emmanuel Amoah said:

Clone the rust-for-linux project (it's a full linux kernel source, with rust features supported)

Rust is part of the core kernel now, so cloning the normal Linux tree from Linus will give you the same thing. But the rust-for-linux repo should also work (after all, it's the same thing).

I found this tutorial for how to use qemu with a custom Linux. I skimmed it and it looks reasonable:
https://www.subrat.info/build-kernel-and-userspace/
Instead of using v5.19 I would pick a more recent version such as v6.17.

I appreciate the find, however I just want to make my needs and constraints very clear:

It looks like the article is about building the generic kernel to run in a vm. From what I've learned, building WITH RUST is the sensitive part, because the generic linux tree apparently doesn't have complete support for it. Therefore, as I stated, it seems your best bet is to get the forked tree in the Rust-for-linux project, which is exactly what I've done. At this point, since I have the modified linux tree which should have all the support I need to build a rust module, I just need to actually do that.

Right now, though it may sound like a noob thing, I just need commands to copy and paste and hope they work, honestly. I'm very tired. I've made clear what I have: I have the latest version of the rust-for-linux tree. I have installed all the components in the documentation. I just need to be able to configure this thing, make this rust-analyzer successfully, and start writing something in vscode. Then, ultimately, build it and run it in a vm, exactly as was done in the video.
I just didn't expect it to be this complicated, man.

Emmanuel Amoah said:

From what I've learned, building WITH RUST is the sensitive part, because the generic linux tree apparently doesn't have complete support for it.

That was true two-three years ago, but it simply is not true today. Everything needed is in the generic linux tree right now.

Emmanuel Amoah said:

I just need to be able to configure this thing, make this rust-analyzer successfully, and start writing something in vscode. Then, ultimately, build it and run it in a vm, exactly as was done in the video.

I can help you with getting rust-analyzer working and the kernel building, but I can't help you with running it in a vm part. Sorry. I'm not using qemu for my development, and I would have to start from scratch to figure out how to do it.

My kernel development either happens in an Android emulator or on real hardware. The emulator requires a special Android checkout, and you would be running an entire Android device in the emulator. Other than that, I run my kernels on real hardware on a physical Pixel phone, or on a Rock5B circuitboard. These are the contexts I know how to do it in, and I recommend that you pick none of those options. Qemu is a better choice for what you're trying to do. I just don't have much experience with running it in qemu.

Cool stuff!
Alright, so no problem. I don't care right now about even running the kernel. I just want to build it, with the rust module. And about the generic tree, sure, but I mean, since I have the rust-for-linux version, that should still work, right? Because, actually, I'm so constrained in my internet usage right now that if I want to download something even half a gigabyte, I'd rather wait till midnight, cos it's way cheaper. Long story.

So in essence, I'm expecting to be able to build a module with this latest version of the rust-for-linux tree. I want to run make LLVM=1 rust-analyzer to generate the config, so I can just write, and BUILD the kernel. Forget about running it. Currently the make LLVM=1 rust-analyzer command gives me:

make[2]: *** No rule to make target 'rust-analyzer'.  Stop.
make[1]: *** [/home/.../rust-for-linux/linux/Makefile:2052: rust-analyzer] Error 2
make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
Can you please try make LLVM=1 rustavailable
Yes, the rust-for-linux tree should work fine too.

Alice Ryhl said:

Can you please try make LLVM=1 rustavailable

Yes, it says Rust is available!
Ok so that means you can enable Rust. Let's make sure it is enabled. Run make LLVM=1 menuconfig and find CONFIG_RUST under "General setup"

Okay, maybe I'm not patient :sweat_smile:, but...
I've entered General setup, I don't seem to find CONFIG_RUST. I used the search, typed CONFIG_RUST, and I'm seeing a lot of things.
Is it supposed to be by a checkbox?

In the "a lot of things" that I'm seeing, I see that it should be under General setup -> Rust support. The number is (1). But when I go to General setup menu, I don't see Rust support in the options.
I know I'm very clueless here. I just watched the menuconfig part of the video to see if I could learn from that. The menuconfig is just confusing me right now
Yeah it is kind of confusing.
If you press / you will be able to search. Try searching for RUST.
image.png

Symbol: RUST [=n]
Type  : bool
Defined at init/Kconfig:2087
  Prompt: Rust support
  Depends on: HAVE_RUST [=n] && RUST_IS_AVAILABLE [=y] && (!MODVERSIONS [=n] || GENDWARFKSYMS [=n]) && !GCC_PLUGIN_RANDS
  Location:
(1) -> General setup
      -> Rust support (RUST [=n])
Selects: EXTENDED_MODVERSIONS [=n] && CFI_ICALL_NORMALIZE_INTEGERS [=n]
The (1) here means that you can press 1 to jump to it.
Ah, so it looks like HAVE_RUST is n. Can you search for HAVE_RUST and show me what it says?
You see my (1) is pointing to the General setup

Alice Ryhl said:

Ah, so it looks like HAVE_RUST is n. Can you search for HAVE_RUST and show me what it says?

Screenshot_20251120_141031.png
Apparently you're building a 32-bit kernel.
Can you search for 64BIT instead?
Hmm... How did that happen, though? What do I do then?
Screenshot_20251120_141337.png
Can you change this option? Try pressing 1 to jump, then toggle it.
I've toggled it
Now search for RUST again
Do I have to Save first?
I don't think so, but you might as well
Okay, I didn't but searching for RUST now put the arrow on Rust support. I now see it and have toggled it. Great!
What next?
Try saving, then build it again with make LLVM=1
I also suspect the rust-analyzer target will work now.
:fingers_crossed: I hope so. It's currently still on make. So when it's done, I run make LLVM=1 rust-analyzer straightaway?
Yep

It worked!
Whew! :sweat_smile:

Alice... Thank you so much for your attention. I couldn't have figured this out any soon. I'll try to continue from here. But if I hit a major block, I'll let you know. Thanks again. I owe you one!
You're welcome.

Hi :wave:
Me again :sweat_smile:

Unfortunately, I've not been able to get autocompletion working in vscode. I have the rust-analyzer plugin enabled. It works for normal Cargo projects, but not with this.
When I open the linux/ folder directly, the plugin recognises the rust-project.json file, whether or not I specify it in the settings.json's rust-analyzer.linkedProjects config.

I see the indicator at the bottom indexing the files and stuff. Yet, when I type, it's like plaintext just with syntax highlighting. I'm wondering if there's some extra setting I need so set or something.
Screenshot_20251120_190736.png
I am not familiar with vscode setups but it shouldn't need indexing when there is rust-project.json file

Onur Özkan said:

I am not familiar with vscode setups but it shouldn't need indexing when there is rust-project.json file

I see. Well... Whichever way, it should still work. I'm not sure why it isn't. At least, the indexing shows me that it is reading the thing. I just don't know why nothing's happening in the editor.

This is because sample-mod.rs is not registered in rust-project.json. The easiest way to just simply make it work is to move your sample-mod.rs into somewhere already registered (e.g., samples/rust/), and modify the Makefile (e.g., samples/rust/Makefile) to target your object file. Then just make LLVM=1 rust-anaylzer again.

You can check scripts/generated_rust_analyzer.py to see which folders are registered:

    # Then, the rest outside of `rust/`.
    #
    # We explicitly mention the top-level folders we want to cover.
    extra_dirs = map(lambda dir: srctree / dir, ("samples", "drivers"))

Thank you for this. In fact, before I saw your message, I had the idea to clone the Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module. With that, you run these:

$ make KDIR=.../linux-with-rust-support LLVM=1
$ make -C .../linux-with-rust-support M=$PWD rust-analyzer

When I did, rust-analyzer worked with the sample module (rust_out_of_tree.rs).

So I get the idea now. I'm just not sure how exactly to modify the Makefile to target my object file like you said. I could either figure it out, or just use the module I just cloned.

I'm a noob with the Makefiles and whole build process thing (soon to change).

The simplest change would be something like this:

diff --git a/samples/rust/Makefile b/samples/rust/Makefile
index cf8422f8f219..1b524eed7601 100644
--- a/samples/rust/Makefile
+++ b/samples/rust/Makefile
@@ -13,6 +13,7 @@ obj-$(CONFIG_SAMPLE_RUST_DRIVER_USB)          += rust_driver_usb.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_SAMPLE_RUST_DRIVER_FAUX)          += rust_driver_faux.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_SAMPLE_RUST_DRIVER_AUXILIARY)     += rust_driver_auxiliary.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_SAMPLE_RUST_CONFIGFS)             += rust_configfs.o
+obj-m          += sample-mod.o

 rust_print-y := rust_print_main.o rust_print_events.o

This will build sample-mod.rs as a module.

Emmanuel Amoah said:

I'm a noob with the Makefiles and whole build process thing (soon to change).

I think a good starting point would be this documentation: https://docs.kernel.org/kbuild/makefiles.html. (I mean, a good starting point for who's already familiar with Makefile syntax but doesn't know how the kernel uses it)
Yess!! That did it for me! Thanks a lot!

Jesung Yang said:

I think a good starting point would be this documentation: https://docs.kernel.org/kbuild/makefiles.html. (I mean, a good starting point for who's already familiar with Makefile syntax but doesn't know how the kernel uses it)

Well... My Makefile syntax is quite basic. I'll have to get more familiar. But the link looks rather explanatory, so definitely bookmarked! Thanks again!
You're welcome :)
Emmanuel Amoah has marked this topic as resolved.
